name: Bharatanatyam
summary: Geometry, rhythm, and expressive storytelling rooted in the Natya Shastra.
description: Known for araimandi posture, intricate footwork, and abhinaya, Bharatanatyam evolved from temple ritual into a global concert form while keeping its devotional core.

name: Kathak
summary: Swift chakkars, tarana, and kathavachan — dance as rhythmic narrative.
description: From storytelling in courts and temples to the tabla-driven virtuosity of today, Kathak balances nrtta (pure dance) with expressive gestures and lightning footwork.

name: Kathakali
summary: Grand costuming, facial codex, and night-long mythic drama.
description: Kathakali synthesizes music, percussion, and heightened gesture; performers train for years in eye and face control before embodying heroes, demons, and gods.

name: Odissi
summary: Tribhangi line, lyrical torso work, and sculptures come to life.
description: Odissi draws from temple relief and mahari tradition; its hallmark is the three-bend stance and flowing, meditative pacing across the stage.

name: Kuchipudi
summary: Lively jatis, drama, and the occasional floor-length solo.
description: Named for the village of Kuchipudi, this form blends brisk technical passages with natya; it can include speech, satire, and demanding rhythmic sequences.

name: Manipuri
summary: Fluid ensemble work, Pung cholom, and Vaishnava ritual aesthetics.
description: Manipuri dance favours continuous, rippling motion and group geometry; the drum and chant anchor both secular stage pieces and temple-linked repertoire.

name: Mohiniyattam
summary: Lasya-forward grace, white-and-gold costume, and swaying repose.
description: Often described as the ‘dance of the enchantress’, Mohiniyattam builds meaning through subtle shifts of weight, eyes, and hands rather than explosive footfire.

name: Sattriya
summary: Monastic origins on the sattra, Eka Sthanika bhangi, and living bhakti.
description: Sattriya was nurtured in Vaishnava monasteries; its repertoire includes ojapali inflections, strict grammar, and an emphasis on devotion over spectacle.
